text: How are you?
Doing great.
How about you?
Very good.
Where are you located?
I'm in beautiful Carmel, Indiana.
That sounds fun.
Is there...
Actually, today was warm.
We got up into the high 40s, so the snow is melting.
Well, yeah, we have roughly 50s, I guess.
High 40s, too, in the area.
Yeah.
Yeah, well, your weather is generally going to be better than mine, unless you're in the city itself enjoying the fog.
I guess, I guess.
But, yeah, very nice to meet you, and thanks for taking the time to chat with us.

My name is Katja, and I'm helping growthx in AI and data science as an advisor, and helping them hiring data science team, as well as here and there, especially in research and running some AI visibility studies as well.
So today, roughly the plan is as follows.
I would love to learn more about you, your recent work.
We'll deep dive in one of your projects.
And then I have a couple of case studies focused on growthx.
I would love to chat through with you.
Does that sound good to you?
Yeah, it sounds really good.
It sounds good.
The point of procedure, though, you had to hear about one of my projects and to do a couple of case studies.
So I'm going to need you to track the time on this, because I don't know how much time you want for each of these.
And just tell me when we're getting close.
Oh, well, sounds good.
Okay.
And I'll also elaborate if I want like a short, high-level details or maybe some more deep dive.
Sounds good.
So maybe I'll start with you.
If you can tell me a little bit about yourself, that would be.
Yeah, so I've always liked building things, and I've got sort of a mathematical bent of mind.
So that, of course, meant building mathematical models of the way the world works, and that's what got me into data science.
Lucky enough, in my PhD, I could apply this to actually understanding how the human brain works.
I was in a neuroscience lab, so playing with the structural MRI, and then decided as long as I'm on big questions, I got to spend my postdoc figuring out how life itself works.
That's grandiose, but I was looking at evolutionary dynamics and how things, you know, look at viruses, which are real-time, and I could see also how they spread through time and space.
So some really nice stuff there, looking at evolutionary patterns and adoption.
Fifteen years in academia from the master's all the way through the end of the postdoc was enough for me to realize I should make some life changes.
So I decided to start my own companies.
Companies?
Yeah, yeah, the first two didn't work out so well.
Yeah.
But by then, I'd kind of figured out a little bit more about what needed to be done.
So the second two actually did work pretty well.
Actually, one of them just got acquired about three months ago.
So that felt nice.
And one of those that worked the best, CyberHedge, I was running that for four years, co-founder.
There's the founder, and I was the first hire.
So I didn't actually found it, but it was him and me for a while, and I did all the technical stuff.
He's non-technical.
Brilliant operator, just non-technical.
After four years, I'd solved the hard problems.
I'd built a solid tech stack that could deliver our solution and the team, more importantly, the team that could actually build and extend that tech stack.
Stripe called me up, and I thought it might be interesting to make a shift, so I did.
Spent a year and a third, year and a half, something like that.

Stripe, but there's a little bit of friction because I'm not used to large organizations and no shade on Stripe, but when you're running a 7,000, 8,000 person company that's in a highly regulated space, you know, I prefer being able to move a little faster and having a little bit more accountability and ownership of my own projects.

So I ended up at Invoca where I am working on mapping out the differences between LLMs and other types of embedding models, which are trained on written word, how, what actually happens when you take these and apply them to spoken word, because we're looking at telephone transcripts and things don't always match up.
So having some fun looking at that intersection right now.
What do they do in Invoca exactly?
I'm staff data scientist on the data team.
No, I mean the, the company.
Oh, company.
Yeah, sorry.
Two things.
The company started by making...
In the link between online advertising and inbound sales calls, let's say you run a dentist office, are you selling some other high-touch product?
You put out your ad on Google, call us to get your children's teeth cleaned, it's the start of your special.
Now your phone starts ringing.
You don't know if it's coming from that Google ad or something else.
We help bridge that gap by rotating phone numbers, so when the person calls, it's one of our numbers, and now we've got the inbound phone calls.
The next question is, okay, so your phone is ringing, but are they calling in response to the ad?
And that's what my team does, is we look at those transcripts and says, what happened on the transcript?
Was it a new patient or was it an existing patient calling to schedule for their kids?
So that's sort of like marketing attribution?
It's exactly marketing attribution, yes.
And it's solving some core issues in marketing attribution.
Okay, so it's basically a provider of marketing attribution models, right?
Yes, yeah, to specializing on things.
Yeah, that's, your summary is good.
And they basically provide that service to any of the company who wants to know their attribution?
Yeah.
In offline, right?
You're saying it's offline?
Yeah.
Obviously, you're not going to buy from us if it doesn't make sense for your company.
But there are a lot of businesses, especially in the U.S., that have rely on inbound telephone calls to close the deal.
Would you say it's more like a small businesses, like in your example?
No.
I gave you the example of a dentist office, but it's not going to be a single dentist running an office.
It's going to be a nationwide chain.
Okay.
So they need to know what works.
I can tell you one of our marquee customers is AutoNation.
I don't know.
What is this?
On AutoNation, they sell cars.
Cars online.
Okay.
So, but, you know, people also call them to sell a car or to buy a car or, you know, buying a car is a kind of a high-tech thing.
And, you know, they want to know if people are calling them.
Are they calling for it to buy the car?
they calling for service?
Are they, you know, why is this person calling us?
And to be able to analyze that at scale.
So they, you basically, it sounds like the company is focusing more on calls rather than.
Yeah.
And everything else.
Because, like, given the company has a billboard, that's not part of UICP, right?
Correct.
Correct.
Yeah, we're focusing on the.
Inbound calls.
Yeah, it's the inbound calls is really our secret sauce.
And that's what we promise.
So if you don't have people calling you, what would be, we'll take your money, but we're not sure what we'll give you in return.
So it's, you know, it's an awkward fit.
It's, it's, it's, it's, it's, know, you
So if you do have those inbound calls, we can do a lot.
And of course, we're looking to grow and, you know, what can we do that expands this and supporting call centers and things.
you know, every company.
big is the company?
350 people.
Oh, and how big is the data team?
Data team is actually really small.
I have two other data scientists on my team.
Then we have two guys who are running the actual platform.
But I have to be a little careful here.
The company's been around for 20, 25 years or so.
So there's also other teams that have built a lot of infrastructure.
We have this massive pipeline.
You know, we have the raw telephone call, basically an MP3 file.
So we're managing the ring switches.
We actually, when you call in, it's going through one of our switches on the telecom network.
That's how we get the transcript.
And so there's the people building that and there's taking the raw audio files.
of people that there's
Turning those into text, that's obviously a data science project, but I don't touch that.
That's not the data science team.
So when I say it's only a few people in the data science, I'm ignoring a lot of people doing a lot of really hard technical data science-y kind of work, but that's, it's, you know, it just helps.
So it's you and another two data scientists?
Yeah, yeah.
Manager is technically really, really competent as well.
So he's not just a good manager, but he's a hands-on data scientist.
That's helpful.
And you guys are like full, full, full stack team doing from analytics to ML.
Interesting definition.
Or is there a separate, separate analytics ML team?
We recently brought in an ML team to help with some of our ML modeling.
So, uh, they're doing, I, these buzzwords get really confusing, Katya.
Uh, ML used to mean one thing and that's...
It's of what I'm doing, but ML now means training an embedding model, and that's what this other team is doing.
So if ML means are you running XGBoost to make predictions, that's one thing.
If you mean setting up a training pipeline to maintain an embedding model in production, that's another thing.
Okay.
So, yeah, we have a team that we brought in to actually manage some of the AI infrastructure in production that we're moving towards.
Our core product is actually a bespoke in-house ML product.
I don't know how deep your knowledge goes.
Obviously, you know what a bag of words model is.
Yeah, yeah.
And you probably heard of latent Dirichlet allocation?
Yes.
Yeah.
Okay, so that's the typical way people do a bag of words.
Yeah.
The Dirichlet prior is interesting, but it doesn't handle fat-tail distributions well.
The better model is a pit manure, which is a variation on the Dirichlet.
And what we have...
is a multi-channel Pitman-Yor model that has like six layers of Bayesian priors on it.
It's a really advanced statistical model, which does make a difference, and that's the model I'm responsible for.
So, yeah, it's basically a backwards-latent Dirichlet allocation, except the stats are much deeper and much more.
Interesting.
Interesting that your core model is Bayesian and core, right?
Yeah.
Well, again, some of this comes down to the difference between spoken and written word.
If you're going to do matching based on similarity, the definition that the embedding model uses for similarity has to match the way people talk.
And if you'll excuse me, the plan, so I was calling about the plan that I was talking about, that's what I want to do, is talk about the plan.
That's how people talk on calls.
Yeah, yeah.
I was cartooning it a little bit, but that is so far from the written word that all the uhs, all the fillers, the fact that they say the plan...
Three times because they're trying to put their thoughts together when they're calling about their insurance plan.
I filed the claim and I'm calling about the claim that I filed.
You know, this is what really happens.
That's not the embedding models miss that.
You have these large gaps between words.
So actually a bag of words model does a better job of filtering out the noise and anything else.
And the other thing is, let's say we want to know if a person is a new patient or an existing patient.
Okay.
That's signal we're looking for on the call.
The call goes for 15 minutes.
There's eight words that distinguish a new from an existing patient on this 15 minutes.
The rest is noise.
So an embedding model is going to try to figure out mostly noise.
A bag of words can actually say, hey, these are the 10 words that matter.
And it doesn't matter how close they are.
There's a lot of room there, you know.
There's lot room there.
There's lot lot
My job right now is experimenting with the better ways of moving this forward, using more embeddings and everything.
It's not a clear win.
How much LLM and GNI do you actually use versus statistical models?
And where do you find it more useful and less useful?
Like one of the examples you gave of a bag of words you find actually a patient's status, very helpful.
But if you can talk more.
also a lot cheaper to run, which also matters.
You know, maybe let's say that the LLM is more accurate, but if it costs you 100x to run it, can you actually affordably run the LLM and everything for a 3% performance gain?
You know, so that comes in.
So to answer your question, some of this, remember, I joined the company a year ago, so I can't be totally responsible for the product stack.
Let's
The company itself had this model.
The team that it created it had just quit.
So they had been a toxic manager that they'd replaced, but not before the damage was done.
And all of this before I joined.
So this is stuff I found out after I joined the company.
My current manager is amazing.
So I really liked the company, did the right thing there.
But basically, they brought me in as the guy who could actually understand this model and make it work.
So that was my first responsibility.
And now the company itself is really making a massive pivot into AI.
That is one of our core things.
Because again, you know, also where the market is going.
So I've been participating in that.
We are looking very strongly at using more AI powered models for doing some of this same analysis.
But it's not quite as straightforward as you initially think, because the spoken is different from the written and the models are trained on the written or the example.
Yes, I can take a telephone transcript, jump into an LLM, and get pretty accurate signal out whether or not they're a new or existing patient.
I know this.
I've tested it.
It obviously works.
It's just too expensive.
Now, could I train a smaller embedding model?
Is it possible to a distilled model that could do the same thing?
Well, before I say yes or no on that, let's pretend that it's a yes to make things easy, and that's such an obvious win.
Great.
Now I've got to convince the company that that's the tech stack that we should build and build this new product, which, again, is doable.
And I can't tell you if that's happening or not because it's proprietary, but you see that's not something that it's like, hey, this is the solution, and it's there in two weeks.
There's time to build that and all the infrastructure around that model.
So even if I do do that and show that it works and show the cost savings or the advantages, I can't say that we have that in production yet.
But, you know, what are some of the ideas?
Like, that's totally understandable, especially if the company was around, you said, 20 years.
It's likely they're moving a little slower, but what are the, you see as a huge potential for a new tech stack as LLM to this problem?
Yeah, so some stuff that does make a huge difference, a lot of our models do use human labeling.

I think that never totally goes away, partly because there's some inertia in our company to keep them, and partly because the LLMs are really good, except on the edge cases, and we actually do need to handle the edge cases.
But we can still leverage LLMs to do the bulk of labeling.
That's one very clear huge win in my own group.
There's a lot of other AI wins across the whole company that I don't want to go, I'll give you a quick example of this.
We're, we'll make an industry report, and we'll specialize this for 10 different industries.
One of our CSMs is really happy that they can use AI to make this process.
So there's stuff like that that happens across the company.
I don't think you're asking about that because I don't touch that.
The CSM is doing that themselves.
But within our own tech stack, certainly the labeling is a clear win.
Some of our other cheaper signals were, you know, I mentioned the gold package, which is you can really customize for basically whatever signal you want to find on the model.
Some of our more out-of-the-box signals, we are moving towards using embedding models in different forms, and that's something I'm deeply involved in.
Of course, everybody wants to build agents.
So if I tell you that we're building agents, that shouldn't be a surprise.
I can't give you details because, again, it's proprietary and public announcements we've made.
But there's some things that an intelligent observer from the outside could see.
What would we offer as our discrimination factor on the agents we'd offer?
Well, we have all the telephone calls, so we know how your human agents handle calls.
And that would allow us to make an AI agent that has the same understanding that your human agents do.
So that's something that the company is exploring quite heavily.
And that, of course, means we'd need to then extract some of this data from these telephone calls to be able to pull it off.
And again, an intelligent outside observer could conclude this fairly easy.
So I'm not telling you anything confidential, but that's some of the projects we're working on that I promise.
Sounds great.
Yeah.
And as I mentioned, I think those are the main things.

If we stick to the current product, what's happening on the phone call, labeling for the basing model when that's appropriate, embedding models for when that's less appropriate, extending new products, as I mentioned, to understand what happens on the calls and turning that into agents.
Is that like a custom for every client is different, what they're trying to do?
up not?
Achieve, or is it like a common across different clients?
What are you trying to get?
Well, let me give you an example.
I already mentioned we work with dentist offices.
So, of course, I can't tell you the national chains we work with, but it doesn't matter because it's a dentist office, right?
We also work with some national chains for insurance companies.
And both of these clients would want to know if the person is a new client or an existing client.
Okay.
So, in a sense, yeah, there are things that are common across all clients.
But the question is, is the model going to be the same?
Because existing a new client is different with a dentist office than it is with an insurance company.
So you would build a different model then for… Yeah.
Well, I mean, again, that's one of the open questions.
On certain signals, we can have out of the box.
We might want to have industry-specific ones.
And that actually is, you know, these are…
Lower tier products are the industry-specific model.
So yeah, you're an insurance company.
We have one that's worked for a lot of insurance companies.
It's set up.
You press go and it goes.
And of course, we can fine-tune one specifically for your data if you pay us a little bit more money and get the gold package instead of the silver package.
Sounds good.
What made you join the company?
And you mentioned that the Stripe was a big company and that you were looking for the switch.
Is that the reason?
Yeah.
Yeah.
And I liked Invoca because, again, it was a unique chance to actually look at the core problem or a core problem with AI is the difference between conversational and written.
I don't know.
I'm not saying that's the biggest business problem out there.
That might not drive the most business value, but scientifically or technically, it's extremely interesting.
And I think that might be – it's a route towards –
What could be a fundamental breakthrough in AI as we move forward?
For Stripe, actually, was it in person in the area or remote?
No, no, it was remote.
Oh, remote, okay.
At Stripe, what are some of the projects you worked on?
Yeah, I had a couple that I was doing.
So the main one was I was embedded in the Miss Billing platform team.
Context on this, let's say you're running your company and you do all your online purchases through Stripe.
You get an invoice from Stripe, you think it's wrong.
You're going to call the Miss Billing team at Stripe to figure out why your invoice isn't what you expect.
So, I mean, it's a level of the B2B level of Miss Billing.
And the initial mandate was based on the assumption...
That if there's a misbilling, it's probably because there's an anomaly in our pricing structure.
So I was looking for anomalies in the pricing structure and assuming that was driving misbilling.
Turns out that there actually aren't that many anomalies.
Stripe is really good.
There had been historically in their grow fast era, they had been a little less rigorous on their pricing, but they tended to catch these things in time before they became catastrophic and fix them.
So that conception turned out to not be totally accurate on what the problem was.
Stripe didn't know this partly because we didn't have good data over what was actually causing the misbilling.
So at first I was looking at detecting anomalies and I developed and delivered some algorithms for that.
And then I started organizing the data on the misbilling board to understand what were actually, what were the different root causes.
What
And the financial impact of each one.
From that work, I realized that one of the biggest drivers of customer pain was actually things that were fairly invisible.
It was our larger clients, enterprise-level clients, who could not predict what their Stripe invoice was because it depended on, for example, changes in the card mix.
And this was very frustrating to clients.
They didn't understand why the same amount of revenue passed through Stripe would have a different invoice each month, invoice amount.
These complaints were also invisible because, again, Stripe is pretty good.
So, I mean, the invoice might be off by $100 here or there.
These are really complex billing sites.
So, you know, being off by $100 is actually amazingly accurate.
But it would take hours or days of time for the poor analyst to figure this out.
And the customer is still upset because the bill was wrong.
You know, and they didn't care if it was $1 million too much or $100,000 too much or $100,000 too little.
It's not the direction.
It's the fact that now they...
Like their accounting is off and they can't predict their costs.
It's just really bad.
So I switched to building an anomaly detector that would see if these bills.
What type of model was it?
It's super simple, super simple, lightweight.
Basically, I was using an autoregressive model, two lags on it.
We're looking at the percentage of revenue.
How much, how many dollars are you passing through Stripe in terms of, you the total card transaction amount and how much we need for that?
Autoregressive time series model?
Yeah, yeah.
Okay.
Super simple.
It was good enough.
It was identifying enough of these big ticket ones that the guy who was dealing with them was happy with it and also pointed in the right direction.
And it was also predictive because one of the other interesting things is it's not so much that these enterprise clients are half stable.
There was clients that suddenly ramped up into enterprise.
And this is a really interesting signal to throw up.
These guys are really wrapping up.
But maybe we wanted to have a heads up that you might get a call from them that their bill isn't quite what they expect.
So that was also – and if you get a phase change in the way the growth trajectory is going with an AR2 model, it's going to be an outlier.
I was flagging some of those as well.
But when we looked at those, those were actually clients that was worth making the CSM aware of because it was going to lead to opportunity.
Yeah.
Strength is generally very, very much known for – as far as I heard, it's a strong team that does a lot of controlled experiments and sophisticated measurement in statistics of their analytics.
Was there anything like that you were working on during this time?
I know exactly what you're talking about.
I forget the name of the staff data scientist who built the experiments platform.
It's called Kepler.
That's the internal Stripe name for it.
I think you work with Paige Bassini.
You can ask her about it so she can give you some details.
I believe she's also at Mercury.
She used to be.
Used to be.
Yeah, okay.
Yeah, that's right.
She did move on, didn't she?
That experiments platform is heavily tied to A-B testing.
And one of the things that Stripe does really, really well is make sure that that checkout service that you're going to embed in your website is smooth.
It's super smooth because of all the A-B testing.
So that's where a lot of the experimentation is focused.
They're also really strong on the fraud detection.
I was able to actually give some advice to one of the fraud guys we brought in because handling extreme distributions was challenging for him.
And I've got some background in extreme distribution theory.
So, yeah, Stripe does have some very sophisticated models.
But simple wins.
I'll go sophisticated if we need to.
And I gave you the example of that in VOCA when we have the pit manure model.
And I didn't go too much into detail, but it's not a pit manure prior.
We have six levels of these pit manure priors, one for the agent track, one for the caller.
And I actually understand and can visualize this in my head.
So, yeah, I can do that.
And, yes, in that case, it's sufficient.
But why do that if you don't need to?
Well, we will talk more about experimentation in metrics during the case study discussion.
But maybe last question to your background.
Maybe let's go a little deeper in one of the projects.
If you can share one of the recent projects you're extremely proud of and just talk me through that.
Yeah.
Well, let's go with one of the ones with the LLM.
And for the labeling, because that's actually.
Topical and hit some of this.
So I said we have this Pitt manure model, which of course is going to, it's semi-supervised.
So we label, let's call it 100 calls, 100 true, 100 false, or 50 true, 50 false, just toy, toy numbers.
This was all done with human labeling.
And we would actually do it in rounds of, they'd label 40 calls, we'd train the model, they'd label another 40 to evaluate it, we'd train the model.
I inherited this, by the way, not saying I designed it that way.
But there's also the initial round zero.
So it took basically about two weeks for a customer to spin up a custom model.
And this is quite annoying to the customers, as you can imagine.
So I took a passion time, we have quarterly passion times.
I forked the production code.
What is passion time?
Oh, it's, you have three days where you can basically, I mean, there's a theme, you're supposed to, this one was built.
Well,
So I forked the production code.

I put an LLM classifier in front of this that would randomly select, randomly start pulling calls and labeling the calls until it had a balanced data set of 100 calls, would train the model, get the model results back, label another 100 calls to evaluate the fit, and then see how that worked.
Then turn it over to human to evaluate it.
So that's an interesting...
Framework, I tested that framework on existing models where we actually had a few hundred labeled human calls.
So I was not using the LLM to evaluate how well it predicted the LLM.

I could hold out the human labeled calls, train the LLM and see how well it predicted on human labeled calls, and then measure model performance, which I was did over, I don't think I got as far as 10, but I was testing over six or seven different clients from very different industry verticals.
And I was able to show that doing this, we should be able to onboard a client in two days instead of two weeks.
And that the LLM, again, I told you earlier that it's struggles with edge cases, but it actually does pretty good for 80 to 90% of the calls.
That's why I know those numbers.
I looked at those calls.
I looked at every single one where the LLM disagreed with the human.
90% of the time the LLM was right, but there were edge cases and there were times where the LLM was just stupid.
And so I was able to show that we could actually use LLMs to measure.
Do you guys deploy the approach?
Well, there's a fun story behind that one.
The short answer is yes, but it took a little bit to get there.
Among other things, are 85 people who did passion time projects, which you want know with 150 or so engineers in the company is roughly what you'd expect.
The CEO, not the CTO, the CEO called out my project.
It was one of only two that we used to demo afterwards at the Nextall Company All Hands.
He said, is the kind of stuff we love.
So definitely got the top-level buy-in from that.
I also told you that the company is pivoting to be AI first.
When I joined, they weren't.
There's a lot of skepticism around AI, and I know you've seen the debate in public, so you know all the arguments.
The C-suite has been working really hard to pivot our company to be an AI first company because they recognize we need to make this change, and there was some institutional inertia.
I think they've done that now.
now.

Watching the Slack channels, I can see now how, I told you about this customer, whoever it was, maybe it was an account executive, but the person generating the report and how they could massively speed their time.
So we've now have the cross-company buy-in that LLMs really are useful and helpful.
And what I didn't tell you is I did this project, they all thought it was great and it got set on the shelf.
And I was thinking, why?
You know, this turns onboarding time from two weeks to two days and removes major point of customer annoyance.
Why aren't we just, and it's off production code.
So it's not hard to slot it into production.
You know, I didn't build this as a toy toy.
It's a fork of the production pipeline.
But they wanted to make sure we had institutional buy-in and now we've got it.
And the project has got greenlit about three weeks ago, two weeks ago.
And the team that manages our customer-facing interfaces is working on building it out.
It took a couple of months, right?
Yeah, it took six months.
Five, six months, four months.
Do you generally actually work together with product managers and execs, or is it far away from your team?
Invoca structure has the data science team is doing more prototyping, stock work, and then we hand over to the other teams to make the production implementation.

I do work really closely with the PM on that prod team because I need to know what his concerns are and his thoughts are to make sure that what we're prototyping matches with where he's taking things.
So, yes, I work very closely with him, and a lot of that is actually just listening to him.
If he has a tech problem, I'm answering it for him, making little tools for him to make his life easier.
So, yes, we work closely with him, but the way that
company is structured, that's actually, it's another team's job to build this out.
And again, would I set up my company that way if I was doing things?
Well, that's really not a question I can ask because I didn't set this company up.
It's how it is.
Yeah.

A lot of times also what I see in my career is like even if you build something super cool that is reducing some time on bringing efficiency or bringing more revenue, it is another part that's bringing it to the crowds and convenience.
How would you like generally go about that and then try to promote and try to execute on the project?
Yeah, yeah.
And you're right.
It's not just me who has this thing.
It's common in the tech world.
So, first of all, you want to make sure that people in the company know you care.
So, when
I told you I work closely with the PM.
He knows that I'm trying to solve his problems.
